questions.text: Gaseous exchange in plants takes place through the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stomata regulate gas exchange between the plant and the environment and control water loss by changing the size of the stomatal pore. This stomatal movement is affected by several environmental stimuli, such as relative humidity, CO2 concentration, and light intensity.

The primary and absolute most important role of mesophyll is its role in photosynthesis. Photosynthesis, as you may know, is the process by which a plant takes carbon dioxide, water, and sunlight and creates sugars, which result in the plant having energy.
The endoderm is the innermost germ layer that forms the linings of the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts, and their associated organs, during embryonic development.
Xylem is the specialized tissue of vascular plants that transports water and nutrients from the plant–soil interface to stems and leaves and provides mechanical support and storage.

questions.explanation: The exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide in the leaf (as well as the loss of water vapor in transpiration) occurs through pores called 'stomata' (singular = stoma). Normally, stomata open when light strikes the leaf in the morning and close during the night.

questions.text: Guard cells function as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Guard cells do not function as the defense system in plants. While plants have various defense mechanisms, such as thorns, toxins, and specialized cells for defense, guard cells primarily regulate the opening and closing of stomata to control gas exchange and water loss.

Guard cells are specialized cells found in the epidermis of leaves that surround and regulate the opening and closing of stomata, which are tiny pores used for gas exchange and act as multisensory hydraulic valves.
While guard cells play a role in maintaining hydrostatic pressure within plant tissues, this is not their primary function. Their main function is the regulation of stomatal opening and closing.
This option is incorrect. Guard cells do not function as the defense system in plants.Their primary function is the regulation of stomatal opening and closing as a multisensory hydraulic valve.

questions.explanation: Guard cells are specialized cells found in the epidermis of leaves that surround and regulate the opening and closing of stomata, which are tiny pores used for gas exchange and act as multisensory hydraulic valves.

questions.text: In which of the following are book lungs found?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Clams are bivalve mollusks, and their respiratory system primarily consists of gills. They have specialized structures called ctenidia or gill filaments that extract oxygen from water.

Spiders possess book lungs, which are unique to this group of arachnids. Book lungs are located in the abdomen and are used for respiration. They consist of a series of thin, membranous plates arranged like the pages of a book, which are richly supplied with blood vessels. Oxygen exchange occurs across these thin plates.

Silverfish, also known as Lepisma, are wingless insects. Unlike spiders, they do not possess book lungs. Silverfish respire through small openings called spiracles found on the sides of their body. These spiracles connect to a network of tubes called tracheae, which distribute oxygen directly to their cells.

Leeches are annelids, which are segmented worms. They do not possess book lungs. Leeches are aquatic or terrestrial organisms and respire through their skin. Oxygen diffuses through their moist body surface into their circulatory system, allowing respiration to occur.

questions.explanation: Book lungs are respiratory organs found in certain arthropods, specifically spiders and some related arachnids. They are not present in the other organisms list. Spiders possess book lungs, which are unique to this group of arachnids. Book lungs are located in the abdomen and are used for respiration. They consist of a series of thin, membranous plates arranged like the pages of a book, which are richly supplied with blood vessels. Oxygen exchange occurs across these thin plates.

questions.text: Smaller the animals:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is correct option because, smaller animals have a higher rate of respiration because they have a higher metabolism. Smaller animals need to generate more heat because their heat loss is greater for their mass. A higher metabolism requires more oxygen and a higher rate of respiration.

Option B is wrong because larger animals have a lower rate of respiration because they have a higher metabolism. Larger animals need to generate less heat because their heat loss is lesser for their mass. A lower metabolism requires less oxygen and a lower rate of respiration.

options.text: Rate of respiration has nothing to do with the size of animal
options.explanation: Option C is incorrect because the rate of respiration is influenced by the size and metabolic rate of the animal. Smaller animals typically have higher respiration rates, while larger animals breathe more slowly to meet their oxygen demands efficiently.
Option D is wrong because option A is correct.

questions.explanation: The larger the organism, more is the distance covered for the circulation of blood inside the body so more time is taken to circulate blood to every part of the body, which will further affect the heartbeat and also lower the breathing rate, so breathing will be deep, but its rate is slow incase of huge animals like a whale. But, incase of smaller organisms, the blood circulation is faster due to a smaller body, eg. a Shrew or a Rat, since the distance to be travelled by circulating blood will be very less…so, breathing rate will be faster here.

questions.text: The chest cavity is bounded by ribs and muscles on the sides, while the floor of the chest cavity is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pleura is a thin membrane with two layers: one covering the lungs (visceral) and one lining the chest wall (parietal). A small amount of fluid between them reduces friction during breathing. It protects the lungs and helps them expand smoothly.

Alveoli are tiny, balloon-like sacs at the ends of the bronchioles in the lungs. They have very thin walls surrounded by capillaries, allowing oxygen to enter the blood and carbon dioxide to leave. Their large surface area makes gas exchange efficient.

The chest cage refers to the bony framework of the thorax, mainly the ribs and sternum. It protects vital organs like the heart and lungs. It also provides attachment points for muscles involved in breathing and movement.

The diaphragm is a large, dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. The chest cavity contains vital organs such as the heart, lungs, and major blood vessels. The floor of the chest cavity is formed by the diaphragm, which contracts and relaxes to facilitate breathing.

questions.explanation: Diaphragm forms the floor of the chest cavity.

questions.text: Vocal cords, which help in voice production are two thin edged fibrous bands, present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The glottis is the space between the vocal cords. While it is closely associated with the vocal cords, it does not specifically refer to the vocal cords themselves. The glottis plays a crucial role in regulating airflow and sound production during speech and other vocalizations. This option is not correct.
While the bronchi are important for respiratory function, they are not directly involved in voice production or the presence of vocal cords. This option is not correct.

The vocal cords are two thin, fibrous bands located in the larynx or voice box, which is a part of the respiratory system. The vocal cords vibrate when air passes through them, producing sound and allowing us to speak.

The trachea, also known as the windpipe, is a tube-like structure that connects the larynx to the bronchi. It provides a pathway for air to enter and exit the lungs. While the trachea is part of the respiratory system, it does not directly involve vocal cord function. This option is not correct.

The pleura is a membrane that surrounds the lungs and lines the chest cavity. It helps to protect and lubricate the lungs, allowing them to expand and contract during respiration. However, it is not directly involved in voice production or the presence of vocal cords. This option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The vocal cords are two thin, fibrous bands located in the larynx or voice box, which is a part of the respiratory system. The vocal cords vibrate when air passes through them, producing sound and allowing us to speak.

questions.text: Lungs are separated from abdominal cavity by a muscular sheath called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pleura is a thin layer of tissue that lines the lungs and the chest cavity. It is a serous membrane, which means that it produces a fluid that helps to lubricate the surfaces of the lungs and the chest cavity. This fluid helps to reduce friction as the lungs move during breathing.

The rib cage is the bony structure that surrounds the lungs. It is made up of 12 pairs of ribs, which are connected to the spine and the sternum. The rib cage helps to protect the lungs and other organs in the chest cavity.

The pericardium is a sac that surrounds the heart. It is made up of two layers: the outer layer is called the fibrous pericardium, and the inner layer is called the serous pericardium. The pericardium helps to protect the heart and to keep it in place.

The lungs are separated from the abdominal cavity by a muscular sheath called the diaphragm. The diaphragm is a dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. It is responsible for breathing, and it helps to move air in and out of the lungs.

questions.explanation: The lungs are separated from the abdominal cavity by a muscular sheath called the diaphragm. The diaphragm is a dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. It is responsible for breathing, and it helps to move air in and out of the lungs.

questions.text: The chest cavity is bounded by ribs and muscles on the sides, while the floor of the chest cavity is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pleura is a thin membrane with two layers: one covering the lungs (visceral) and one lining the chest wall (parietal). A small amount of fluid between them reduces friction during breathing. It protects the lungs and helps them expand smoothly.

Alveoli are tiny, balloon-like sacs at the ends of the bronchioles in the lungs. They have very thin walls surrounded by capillaries, allowing oxygen to enter the blood and carbon dioxide to leave. Their large surface area makes gas exchange efficient.

The chest cage refers to the bony framework of the thorax, mainly the ribs and sternum. It protects vital organs like the heart and lungs. It also provides attachment points for muscles involved in breathing and movement.

The diaphragm is a large, dome-shaped muscle that separates the chest cavity from the abdominal cavity. The chest cavity contains vital organs such as the heart, lungs, and major blood vessels. The floor of the chest cavity is formed by the diaphragm, which contracts and relaxes to facilitate breathing.

questions.explanation: Diaphragm forms the floor of the chest cavity.

questions.text: Which vertebrate organ receives only oxygenated blood?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The spleen is a vital organ in the vertebrate circulatory system which only recieves oxygenated blood with several functions , including:Blood Filtration: One of the key functions of the spleen is to filter blood. It helps remove damaged, old, or abnormal blood cells, including red blood cells and platelets. By doing this, it contributes to maintaining the quality of circulating blood.Reservoir for Blood: The spleen serves as a reservoir for blood. It can store a significant volume of blood, which can be released into circulation when needed. This can be particularly important in situations like hemorrhage or increased demand for blood supply.Immune Functions: The spleen plays a role in the immune system. It contains white blood cells, including lymphocytes, which are involved in immune responses. In response to infections or the presence of foreign materials in the blood, the spleen can produce immune responses to defend against pathogens.Platelet Storage: The spleen stores a portion of the body's platelets (thrombocytes). Platelets are essential for blood clotting, and the spleen's ability to release stored platelets can be important in managing bleeding.It's important to note that while the spleen is involved in the circulatory system and has several crucial functions related to blood, it does primarily receive only oxygenated blood. But, its functions are more centered around blood filtration, storage, and immune responses. Oxygenated blood flows into various organs and tissues, including the spleen.

The liver is a crucial organ with various functions, but it does not receive only oxygenated blood. The liver receives blood from two sources: the hepatic artery and the hepatic portal vein. The hepatic artery carries oxygenated blood from the heart to supply the liver with oxygen. In contrast, the hepatic portal vein carries nutrient-rich, deoxygenated blood from the digestive organs. The liver plays essential roles in metabolism, detoxification, and producing various substances, such as bile.

Gills are the respiratory organs of aquatic animals, such as fish. They play a vital role in extracting oxygen from water and releasing carbon dioxide. While gills receive oxygenated water, they also receive deoxygenated blood for gas exchange. Oxygen from the water diffuses into the bloodstream, while carbon dioxide from the blood diffuses into the water. This process ensures that the fish obtain oxygen and eliminate carbon dioxide.

Lung : The pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation. In the lungs, oxygen is taken up from the air and binds to hemoglobin in red blood cells, while carbon dioxide is released from the blood into the alveoli to be exhaled. The oxygenated blood then returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins.

questions.explanation: All options are explained below.Option A is correct.a)Spleen: The spleen is a vital organ in the vertebrate circulatory system which only recieves oxygenated blood with several functions , including:Blood Filtration: One of the key functions of the spleen is to filter blood. It helps remove damaged, old, or abnormal blood cells, including red blood cells and platelets. By doing this, it contributes to maintaining the quality of circulating blood.Reservoir for Blood: The spleen serves as a reservoir for blood. It can store a significant volume of blood, which can be released into circulation when needed. This can be particularly important in situations like hemorrhage or increased demand for blood supply.Immune Functions: The spleen plays a role in the immune system. It contains white blood cells, including lymphocytes, which are involved in immune responses. In response to infections or the presence of foreign materials in the blood, the spleen can produce immune responses to defend against pathogens.Platelet Storage: The spleen stores a portion of the body's platelets (thrombocytes). Platelets are essential for blood clotting, and the spleen's ability to release stored platelets can be important in managing bleeding.It's important to note that while the spleen is involved in the circulatory system and has several crucial functions related to blood, it does primarily receive only oxygenated blood. But, its functions are more centered around blood filtration, storage, and immune responses. Oxygenated blood flows into various organs and tissues, including the spleen.b)Liver: The liver is a crucial organ with various functions, but it does not receive only oxygenated blood. The liver receives blood from two sources: the hepatic artery and the hepatic portal vein. The hepatic artery carries oxygenated blood from the heart to supply the liver with oxygen. In contrast, the hepatic portal vein carries nutrient-rich, deoxygenated blood from the digestive organs. The liver plays essential roles in metabolism, detoxification, and producing various substances, such as bile.c)Gill: Gills are the respiratory organs of aquatic animals, such as fish. They play a vital role in extracting oxygen from water and releasing carbon dioxide. While gills receive oxygenated water, they also receive deoxygenated blood for gas exchange. Oxygen from the water diffuses into the bloodstream, while carbon dioxide from the blood diffuses into the water. This process ensures that the fish obtain oxygen and eliminate carbon dioxide.d)Lung : The pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation. In the lungs, oxygen is taken up from the air and binds to hemoglobin in red blood cells, while carbon dioxide is released from the blood into the alveoli to be exhaled. The oxygenated blood then returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins.Summary:Spleen: The spleen is involved in blood filtration, storage, and immune functions. It acts as a reservoir for blood and removes damaged blood cells and it primarily receive only oxygenated blood.Liver: The liver receives both oxygenated blood from the hepatic artery and deoxygenated, nutrient-rich blood from the hepatic portal vein. It is responsible for various metabolic and detoxification functions.Gill: Gills are the respiratory organs of aquatic animals and play a role in extracting oxygen from water and releasing carbon dioxide. They receive oxygenated water but also receive deoxygenated blood for gas exchange.Lung. It plays a central role in oxygen exchange, as deoxygenated blood from the heart is sent to the lungs to pick up oxygen and release carbon dioxide.

questions.text: What is true for mammalia?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is true. The platypus is one of the few mammals that lay eggs (oviparous). However, it does not directly relate to the respiratory anatomy or mechanics of how mammals ventilate their lungs.

This statement is false. Bats are mammals characterized by fur, not feathers. They use their diaphragm and intercostal muscles to facilitate ventilation, which is crucial for their flight and respiration.

This statement is incorrect. Elephants are viviparous, meaning they give birth to live young instead of laying eggs or being ovoviviparous. The diaphragm is a key muscle in their respiratory system, aiding in effective lung ventilation.

This statement is false. The diaphragm is present in mammals and is essential for respiration, contracting to increase the volume of the thoracic cavity and allowing for lung expansion during inhalation.

questions.explanation: The platypus is a monotreme, a unique group of mammals that lay eggs (oviparous) instead of giving birth to live young. Monotremes still produce milk to feed their offspring, like other mammals. The other options are incorrect: bats have fur, not feathers; elephants are viviparous, not ovoviviparous; and mammals possess a diaphragm.

questions.text: Respiratory system develops from :-
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The respiratory system develops from the endoderm, which is one of the three primary germ layers formed during embryonic development. The endoderm gives rise to various internal organs, including the lining of the respiratory tract and lungs.
Ectoderm gives rise to the skin, nervous system, and other external structures, not the respiratory system.
Mesoderm gives rise to structures like muscles, bones, and some organs but not the respiratory system.
This option is also incorrect because respiratory system develops from endoderm.

questions.explanation: The respiratory system develops from the endoderm, which is one of the three primary germ layers formed during embryonic development. The endoderm gives rise to various internal organs, including the lining of the respiratory tract and lungs.

questions.text: The volume of air remain in lungs even after very deep breath is :-
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tidal volume is the amount of air breathed in or out during normal, quiet breathing.
This is the volume of air that remains in the lungs even after a very deep breath. It ensures the lungs stay partially inflated and prevents their collapse.
Reserved volume is not a recognized term in respiratory physiology.
Dead space refers to the air that occupies the space in the respiratory system that does not participate in gas exchange, such as the trachea and bronchi.

questions.explanation: This is the volume of air that remains in the lungs even after a very deep breath. It ensures the lungs stay partially inflated and prevents their collapse.

questions.text: In human , the epithelium which separates air and blood is only
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the epithelium separating air and blood (found in the alveoli) is typically more than one cell thick.
The epithelium separating air and blood in the alveoli is typically two cell layers thick, consisting of alveolar cells and endothelial cells that line the capillaries.
This option is incorrect; it's usually two cell layers thick as mentioned above.
This option is incorrect; the separation is generally two cell layers thick.

questions.explanation: The epithelium separating air and blood in the alveoli is typically two cell layers thick, consisting of alveolar cells and endothelial cells that line the capillaries.

questions.text: In glottis, mucous membrane is stretched across into
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: C shaped cartilaginous rings are found in the trachea, not in the glottis.
The epiglottis is a flap-like structure that prevents food from entering the trachea during swallowing, but it's not a stretched mucous membrane.
The larynx contains the vocal cords, but the stretched mucous membrane is not associated with the entire larynx.
The glottis is the part of the larynx containing the vocal cords, which are made of two thin, stretched, fibrous bands. They vibrate to produce sound when air passes through them.

questions.explanation: The glottis is the part of the larynx containing the vocal cords, which are made of two thin, stretched, fibrous bands. They vibrate to produce sound when air passes through them.

questions.text: Oxyhaemoglobin will dissociate when
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because a lower pH (acidic conditions) due to increased carbon dioxide levels (e.g., in tissues during metabolism) encourages oxygen release from oxyhemoglobin, aiding oxygen delivery to cells.
This is not a significant factor for dissociation. Oxygen dissociation curve shows that lower PO2 mainly affects oxygen binding, not dissociation.
Higher temperature promotes dissociation. Lower temperature does not hinder dissociation.
Carbonic anhydrase is involved in carbon dioxide transport, not in oxygen dissociation.

questions.explanation: This option is correct because a lower pH (acidic conditions) due to increased carbon dioxide levels (e.g., in tissues during metabolism) encourages oxygen release from oxyhemoglobin, aiding oxygen delivery to cells.

questions.text: Arterial blood contains _ of CO2 per 100 ml of blood
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This value is closer to the CO2 content found in venous blood, which is typically higher than arterial blood.
This value exceeds the typical CO2 content found in arterial blood and is more representative of venous blood.
This value is significantly higher than what is typically found in arterial blood and likely represents venous blood levels.
Arterial blood generally contains around 50ml of CO2 per 100ml. This reflects the lower CO2 concentration in arterial blood compared to venous blood.

questions.explanation: Arterial blood contains approximately 50ml of CO2 per 100ml, reflecting its role in transporting oxygenated blood with lower CO2 levels compared to venous blood. Venous blood, having returned from the body's tissues, carries more CO2 as a waste product, hence the higher values found in other options.

questions.text: All of the following contain cartilage except
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The larynx contains cartilage in the form of several structures, including the thyroid cartilage (Adam's apple) and the cricoid cartilage.
The trachea contains rings of cartilage to provide structural support and prevent collapse.
Bronchioles are smaller air passages that do not contain cartilage. They have smooth muscle instead.
The bronchi are larger air passages that do contain cartilage to maintain their shape and structure.

questions.explanation: Bronchioles are smaller air passages that do not contain cartilage. They have smooth muscle instead.

questions.text: Of the following, which one is a contagious disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tuberculosis (TB) is a contagious bacterial infection that primarily affects the lungs but can also spread to other parts of the body.
Lung cancer is not contagious; it is caused by uncontrolled cell growth and is typically related to factors like smoking, exposure to certain substances, and genetic factors.
Asthma is not contagious. It is a chronic respiratory condition caused by inflammation of the airways.
Sinusitis is not contagious. It is an inflammation of the sinuses often caused by infections, allergies, or other factors.

questions.explanation: Tuberculosis (TB) is a contagious bacterial infection that primarily affects the lungs but can also spread to other parts of the body.

questions.text: The maximum amount of oxygen which normal human blood absorbs and carries at sea level is _ per 100 ml of blood.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 10 ml → ❌ Too low; actual oxygen content is higher.

At sea level, normal human blood can absorb and carry approximately 20ml of oxygen per 100ml of blood. This value can vary based on factors like hemoglobin concentration, altitude, and physical activity.
30 ml → ❌ Too high, exceeds normal carrying capacity.
40 ml → ❌ Much too high, not physiologically possible.

questions.explanation: At sea level, normal human blood can absorb and carry approximately 20ml of oxygen per 100ml of blood. This value can vary based on factors like hemoglobin concentration, altitude, and physical activity.

questions.text: Lungs are spongy due to the presence of million of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Air sacs are not a specific component that makes the lungs spongy. Alveoli, which are found within the air sacs, contribute to the spongy texture.
Lungs are spongy due to the presence of millions of tiny air sacs called alveoli. These alveoli are responsible for the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide during the process of respiration.
Bronchi are larger air passages that do not contribute to the spongy texture of the lungs.
Hairs (cilia) are present in the respiratory tract and serve to move mucus, but they do not give the lungs their spongy texture.

questions.explanation: Lungs are spongy due to the presence of millions of tiny air sacs called alveoli. These alveoli are responsible for the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide during the process of respiration.

questions.text: Breathing consists of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Breathing does not typically consist of four distinct phases.
While there are different aspects to the breathing process, it's not typically divided into three phases.
This option is correct because breathing generally involves two main phases: inhalation (inspiration) and exhalation (expiration).
Breathing involves more than one phase.

questions.explanation: This option is correct because breathing generally involves two main phases: inhalation (inspiration) and exhalation (expiration).

questions.text: The total inside capacity of lungs
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This value is above the typical range for total lung capacity, which is around 5 liters.
This value is slightly above the average total lung capacity for most adults, which is roughly 5 liters.
This is the average total lung capacity for a healthy adult, making it the correct choice.
This value is lower than the typical total lung capacity of an adult, which is approximately 5 liters.

questions.explanation: The total lung capacity of an adult is the maximum amount of air the lungs can hold, which is typically around 5 liters. This accounts for the volume of air in the lungs after a full inhalation. Option C (5 liters) is correct because it reflects this average capacity. Options A (7 liters) and B (6 liters) are incorrect because they exceed the typical capacity. Option D (4 liters) is too low, underestimating the average total lung capacity.

questions.text: Breakdown of alveoli wall take place in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Emphysema is a condition where the walls of the alveoli break down, leading to reduced lung function and difficulty in breathing.
Asthma involves inflammation and narrowing of the airways, but it doesn't necessarily lead to the breakdown of alveoli walls.
Tuberculosis is a bacterial infection that primarily affects the lungs but doesn't directly lead to the breakdown of alveoli walls.
Cancer can cause abnormal growths in the lungs, but it's not typically associated with the breakdown of alveoli walls.

questions.explanation: Emphysema is a condition where the walls of the alveoli break down, leading to reduced lung function and difficulty in breathing.

questions.text: Exhaled air contain _ % CO2.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the approximate percentage of carbon dioxide (CO2) in the Earth's atmosphere, not in exhaled air. In exhaled air, CO2 is significantly higher.
This is the percentage of oxygen in the atmosphere, not the percentage of CO2 in exhaled air. Exhaled air has much less oxygen and more CO2.
This percentage is incorrect for CO2 in exhaled air. It is closer to the oxygen content in exhaled air, which is about 16%, not CO2.
This option is correct because exhaled air typically contains around 4% carbon dioxide (CO2), which is much higher than the atmospheric level.

questions.explanation: Exhaled air contains approximately 4% carbon dioxide (CO2), which is significantly higher than the 0.04% found in the Earth's atmosphere. This is because the respiratory system exchanges oxygen for carbon dioxide during the process of gas exchange. Option A (0.04) refers to atmospheric CO2 levels, Option B (21) is the atmospheric oxygen percentage, and Option C (16) is closer to the oxygen content in exhaled air rather than CO2.

questions.text: Low partial pressure of oxygen in tissue favours the _ of oxyhaemoglobin.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Low partial pressure of oxygen doesn't favor the stability of oxyhemoglobin; instead, it encourages the release of oxygen.

This option is correct because when there's a low partial pressure of oxygen in tissue, it promotes the dissociation of oxygen from oxyhemoglobin, allowing oxygen to be released and supplied to the tissues.
Oxyhemoglobin forms when oxygen binds to hemoglobin. Low oxygen levels would not encourage the formation of oxyhemoglobin.
Low partial pressure of oxygen does not directly relate to the transport of oxyhemoglobin; it is more relevant to the dissociation of oxygen from hemoglobin.

questions.explanation: This option is correct because when there's a low partial pressure of oxygen in tissue, it promotes the dissociation of oxygen from oxyhemoglobin, allowing oxygen to be released and supplied to the tissues.

questions.text: Respiratory tubules are known as bronchioles when they attains diameter of .
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This diameter is too large for bronchioles.
This diameter is also too large for bronchioles.
This diameter is slightly larger than what bronchioles typically attain.

Respiratory tubules are known as bronchioles when they attain a diameter of around 1 mm. This is a significant reduction in size from the larger airways like bronchi, as the respiratory tree branches into smaller passages within the lungs.

questions.explanation: Respiratory tubules are known as bronchioles when they attain a diameter of around 1 mm. This is a significant reduction in size from the larger airways like bronchi, as the respiratory tree branches into smaller passages within the lungs.

questions.text: Which is not true about human lungs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is not entirely accurate. Lungs are not "open" sacs in the sense of having open cavities, but they have a highly intricate structure with many air sacs (alveoli) where gas exchange occurs.
This statement is not true. Lungs are not "closed" sacs; they are connected to the external environment through the airways, allowing for the exchange of air and gases.
This statement is true. Lungs have a spongy appearance due to the numerous air-filled alveoli that make up their structure.

options.text: They are placed in chest cavity
options.explanation: This statement is true. Lungs are located in the chest cavity (thoracic cavity) and are protected by the ribcage.

questions.explanation: The lungs are not 'open' sacs; they are complex structures with numerous alveoli that facilitate gas exchange. While connected to the external environment via airways, their internal structure is not an open space.

questions.text: The smaller tubes within the chest cavity having cartilaginous plates are known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pharynx is a muscular tube-shaped structure that connects the nasal cavity and mouth to the esophagus and larynx. It is part of both the respiratory and digestive systems and plays a crucial role in the passage of air and food.
Bronchioles are small tubes in the lungs that branch off from the bronchi and divide further into even smaller tubes called alveolar ducts.
The bronchi are the main passageways that carry air from the trachea into the lungs, where they further branch into smaller tubes called bronchioles.
Bronchi are supported by cartilage rings, while bronchioles have no cartilage. Instead, they are supported by smooth muscle.

questions.explanation: The pharynx is a muscular tube-shaped structure that connects the nasal cavity and mouth to the oesophagus and larynx. It is part of both the respiratory and digestive systems and plays a crucial role in the passage of air and food.Bronchioles are small tubes in the lungs that branch off from the bronchi and divide further into even smaller tubes called alveolar ducts. The bronchi are the large airways that connect the trachea to the lungs.Bronchi are supported by cartilage Plates while bronchioles have no cartilage. Instead, they are supported by smooth muscle.

questions.text: The epiglottis a flap of tissues covers the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pharynx is a shared pathway for both respiratory and digestive systems. The epiglottis does not specifically cover the pharynx, but plays a role in directing food and air in the correct pathways.

The larynx is the section of the respiratory tract immediately below the pharynx and above the trachea. It houses the vocal cords and prevents food from entering the trachea, but the epiglottis specifically covers the glottis, part of the larynx, during swallowing.
The glottis is the opening between the vocal cords in the larynx. The epiglottis covers the glottis during swallowing to prevent food and drink from entering the trachea.
The nasal cavity is involved in filtering and humidifying the air we breathe, but it is not related to the function of the epiglottis. The epiglottis does not cover the nasal cavity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Glottis. The epiglottis is a flap of tissue that covers the glottis during swallowing to prevent food and liquids from entering the trachea. The pharynx is a shared path for food and air but is not specifically covered by the epiglottis. The larynx contains the glottis and is protected by the epiglottis during swallowing, but the epiglottis directly covers the glottis. The nasal cavity is unrelated to the function of the epiglottis as it deals with air filtration and olfaction.

questions.text: In nostrils, the substance which moistens and keep the incoming air warm is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The bronchi are the large airways that branch from the trachea and enter each lung; they contain cartilage, smooth muscle, and a mucous-lined epithelium. Their main roles are to conduct air to and from the lungs and to participate in mucociliary clearance deeper in the airway. Although the bronchi are lined with mucus-secreting cells and cilia, they are located well below the nostrils and are not the primary structures that condition (moisten and warm) air at the point of entry. Thus, while bronchi help protect the lower airway, they do not perform the nostril-level humidifying/warming function named in the question.

The nasal cavity’s lining (nasal mucosa) contains goblet cells and seromucous glands that secrete mucus — a viscous fluid made of water, mucins (glycoproteins), enzymes (e.g., lysozyme), and antibodies (especially secretory IgA). Mucus traps dust, pollen, microbes and adds moisture to inspired air so the respiratory tract and alveoli do not dry out. Together with the rich capillary network in the nasal conchae (turbinates), mucus and blood flow warm the air toward body temperature. Cilia on the respiratory epithelium sweep the mucus (with trapped particles) posteriorly into the pharynx to be swallowed, which keeps the airways clean. Because mucus both humidifies and helps maintain temperature of the incoming air at the nostril level, it is the correct answer.

The pharynx is the shared throat space behind the nasal and oral cavities (divided into nasopharynx, oropharynx, laryngopharynx) that serves as a conduit for both air and food. Its lining includes mucous membranes and lymphoid tissue (tonsils), but the pharynx mainly functions as a passage and does not perform the primary humidifying/warming role — that job is done earlier in the nasal cavity. Also, “pharynx duct” is not a standard anatomical term; if the intention was “pharynx,” it remains an incorrect choice for the specific nostril-level conditioning function.

The glottis is the slit-like opening between the vocal cords within the larynx; it regulates airflow into the trachea and is essential for phonation and airway protection (the epiglottis closes over it during swallowing). It is lined by mucous membrane like other airway structures, but it sits lower than the nasal cavity and does not serve to humidify or warm inhaled air at the entry point. Its role is mechanical (control of airflow and sound) and protective, not primary air conditioning.

questions.explanation: When you inhale, the air is first conditioned in the nasal cavity so it reaches the lungs at an appropriate temperature and humidity and free of large particles. The nostrils and nasal cavity have hairs (vibrissae) that block large debris, and the convoluted nasal conchae produce turbulence so air contacts a large, well-vascularized mucous surface. Goblet cells and seromucous glands secrete mucus that humidifies the air and traps particles and microbes; enzymes and IgA in the mucus help neutralize pathogens. The dense capillary plexus in the nasal mucosa transfers heat to the inhaled air, warming it toward body temperature, and cilia move the mucus with trapped debris toward the pharynx to be cleared. Because of these combined features, mucus (from the nasal mucosa) is the primary substance in the nostrils that moistens and helps keep incoming air warm, protecting the delicate lower respiratory tract and optimizing conditions for gas exchange.

questions.text: Each nasal cavity is subdivided into _ passageways in man.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is wrong because it is a fact. Each nasal cavity is not subdivided into 1 passageway. Each nasal cavity is subdivided into three passageways by the projections of bones from the wall of the internal nose. The nasal cavity is divided into vestibule, respiratory and olfactory sections

This option is wrong because each nasal cavity is not sub-divided into 2 passage ways. Each nasal cavity is subdivided into three passageways by the projections of bones from the wall of the internal nose. The nasal cavity is divided into vestibule, respiratory and olfactory sections
Each nasal cavity is subdivided into three passageways by the projections of bones from the wall of the internal nose. The nasal cavity is divided into vestibule, respiratory and olfactory sections.
This option is incorrect is wrong because each nasal cavity is sub divided into 3 passage ways instead of 4.

questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because it is a fact. Each nasal cavity is not subdivided into 1 passageway.Option B is wrong because each nasal cavity is not sub-divided into 2 passageways. Option: CEach nasal cavity is subdivided into three passageways by the projections of bones from the wall of the internal nose. PTB XI Chap # 13 page # 267 The nasal cavity is divided into vestibule, respiratory and olfactory sections. (Fact) Option D is wrong because each nasal cavity is subdivided into 3 passageways instead of 4

questions.text: The figure given below shows a small part of the human lung where exchange of gases takes place. Select the option that represents the labeled part (A, B, C, or D) correctly, along with its function:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Alveolar cavity - main site of exchange of respiratory gases
options.explanation: The alveolar cavity is indeed the primary site of gas exchange in the lungs. This is where oxygen is taken up by the blood from inhaled air, and carbon dioxide is released from the blood to be exhaled. The alveoli are tiny, thin-walled air sacs within the lungs, and they provide a large surface area for efficient gas exchange.

options.text: Red blood cells transport CO2 mainly
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Red blood cells (RBCs) play a crucial role in the transport of carbon dioxide (CO2). They carry CO2 from body tissues to the lungs, where it can be exhaled.

options.text: Arterial capillary - passes oxygen to tissues
options.explanation: Arterial capillaries are part of the circulatory system and are responsible for delivering oxygen-rich blood to body tissues, not for "passing oxygen to tissues." Oxygen is released from the blood in the tissues and taken up by the cells. The statement does not accurately describe the function of arterial capillaries.

options.text: Capillary wall - decrease surface area for exchange of O2 and CO2
options.explanation: Capillaries, including the capillary walls, serve the vital function of facilitating the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the blood and the surrounding tissues. They do not decrease the surface area for gas exchange but rather maximize it.

questions.explanation: Option a is correct.All options are explained below.a)Alveolar cavity - main site of exchange of respiratory gases (Correct Option):The alveolar cavity is indeed the primary site of gas exchange in the lungs. This is where oxygen is taken up by the blood from inhaled air, and carbon dioxide is released from the blood to be exhaled. The alveoli are tiny, thin-walled air sacs within the lungs, and they provide a large surface area for efficient gas exchange.B: Red blood cells transport CO2 mainly: This statement is accurate. Red blood cells (RBCs) play a crucial role in the transport of carbon dioxide (CO2). They carry CO2 from body tissues to the lungs, where it can be exhaled.C: Arterial capillary - passes oxygen to tissues: Arterial capillaries are part of the circulatory system and are responsible for delivering oxygen-rich blood to body tissues, not for "passing oxygen to tissues." Oxygen is released from the blood in the tissues and taken up by the cells. The statement does not accurately describe the function of arterial capillaries.D: Capillary wall - decrease surface area for exchange of O2 and CO2: Capillaries, including the capillary walls, serve the vital function of facilitating the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the blood and the surrounding tissues. They do not decrease the surface area for gas exchange but rather maximize it.Summary: The correct option is A, which correctly identifies the labeled part as the alveolar cavity, the primary site of gas exchange in the lungs. The other options either describe incorrect functions or are not accurate representations of the labeled structures in the diagram.

questions.text: Listed below are four respiratory capacities (i–iv) and four jumbled respiratory volumes of a normal human adult. Which one of the following is the correct matching of two capacities and volumes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a)(ii) 2500 mL, (iii) 4500 mL:This option pairs capacity (ii) with 2500 mL and capacity (iii) with 4500 mL. This is an incorrect match. It doesn't align with the jumbled respiratory volumes provided in the question.
B. (iii) 1200 mL, (iv) 2500 mL: This option pairs capacity (iii) with 1200 mL and capacity (iv) with 2500 mL. It also accurately pairs the capacities with their respective volumes.

(iv) 3500 mL, (i) 1200 mL (Correct Option):This option pairs capacity (iv) with 3500 mL and capacity (i) with 1200 mL. If this is the correct option, it means that capacity (i) has a volume of 1200 mL, and capacity (iv) has a volume of 3500 mL, as per the question.

(i) 4500 mL, (ii) 3500 mL:This option pairs capacity (i) with 4500 mL and capacity (ii) with 3500 mL. This is an incorrect match and doesn't align with the jumbled respiratory volumes provided in the question.

questions.explanation: All options are explained below:a)(ii) 2500 mL, (iii) 4500 mL:This option pairs capacity (ii) with 2500 mL and capacity (iii) with 4500 mL. This is an incorrect match. It doesn't align with the jumbled respiratory volumes provided in the question.B. (iii) 1200 mL, (iv) 2500 mL: This option pairs capacity (iii) with 1200 mL and capacity (iv) with 2500 mL. It also accurately pairs the capacities with their respective volumes.C. (iv) 3500 mL, (i) 1200 mL (Correct Option):This option pairs capacity (iv) with 3500 mL and capacity (i) with 1200 mL. If this is the correct option, it means that capacity (i) has a volume of 1200 mL, and capacity (iv) has a volume of 3500 mL, as per the question.D. (i) 4500 mL, (ii) 3500 mL:This option pairs capacity (i) with 4500 mL and capacity (ii) with 3500 mL. This is an incorrect match and doesn't align with the jumbled respiratory volumes provided in the question.Summary: The correct match is option C, which pairs capacity (iv) with 3500 mL and capacity (i) with 1200 mL, as indicated by the jumbled respiratory volumes provided in the question. The other options provide incorrect pairings of respiratory volumes with capacities.

questions.text: What is vital capacity of our lungs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Inspiratory reserve volume plus expiratory reserve volume
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it does not include tidal volume, which is also a part of vital capacity.

options.text: Total lung capacity minus residual volume
options.explanation: Correct. Vital capacity is calculated by subtracting the residual volume from the total lung capacity.

options.text: Inspiratory reserve volume plus tidal volume
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it is missing the expiratory reserve volume, which is also part of vital capacity.

options.text: Total lung capacity minus expiratory reserve volume
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it does not accurately represent the formula for vital capacity.

questions.explanation: The vital capacity of the lungs is the maximum amount of air a person can expel from the lungs after a maximum inhalation. It is represented by the sum of the tidal volume, inspiratory reserve volume, and expiratory reserve volume. This means vital capacity is total lung capacity minus the residual volume. Therefore, the correct option is B. Option A is incorrect because it does not include tidal volume. Option C is incorrect because it omits expiratory reserve volume. Option D is incorrect as it does not follow the correct formula for vital capacity.

questions.text: The exchange of gases in the alveoli of the lungs takes place by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Passive transport includes processes like diffusion and osmosis, where substances move across membranes without energy input. Although diffusion is a form of passive transport, this term is too broad to specify the exact process occurring in the alveoli.
Active transport requires energy to move substances against their concentration gradient. Gas exchange in the alveoli does not require energy input, making active transport an incorrect choice.
Osmosis is the movement of water across a semi-permeable membrane. Gas exchange involves oxygen and carbon dioxide, not water, so osmosis is not the correct process.

Simple diffusion is the movement of molecules from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration without energy input. This is the process by which oxygen and carbon dioxide are exchanged in the alveoli.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is simple diffusion. In the alveoli, oxygen diffuses from the alveolar air, where its concentration is higher, into the blood, where its concentration is lower. Similarly, carbon dioxide diffuses from the blood, where its concentration is higher, into the alveolar air, where its concentration is lower. This process occurs due to differences in partial pressures of the gases and does not require energy.Other options are incorrect because:Passive transport: Although diffusion is a form of passive transport, this term does not specify the exact process occurring in the alveoli.Active transport: This process requires energy, which is not involved in the natural gas exchange in alveoli.Osmosis: This involves water movement, not gases like oxygen and carbon dioxide.

questions.text: Vacuum is created inside lungs when
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When the intercostal muscles contract, they pull the ribcage upward and outward, expanding the chest cavity. This causes a decrease in pressure inside the lungs, creating a partial vacuum. Air then rushes into the lungs to equalize the pressure, leading to inhalation.
Lungs being pressed would not create a vacuum but rather lead to a reduction in lung volume and exhalation.
When the diaphragm relaxes, it moves upward, reducing the size of the chest cavity and leading to exhalation. It doesn't create a vacuum inside the lungs.

Surfactant is a substance that reduces surface tension in the alveoli, preventing them from collapsing. While a deficiency in surfactant can lead to respiratory issues, it doesn't directly create a vacuum inside the lungs.

questions.explanation: When the intercostal muscles contract, they pull the ribcage upward and outward, expanding the chest cavity. This causes a decrease in pressure inside the lungs, creating a partial vacuum. Air then rushes into the lungs to equalize the pressure, leading to inhalation.

questions.text: Which pair is incorrect:-
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Normally, the tidal volume is around 500 ml. Tidal volume refers to the volume of air inhaled and exhaled during normal breathing.
The residual volume is the amount of air left in the lungs after maximum exhalation, and it is typically around 1200 ml.
Vital capacity is the maximum amount of air a person can exhale after a maximum inhalation. It is usually around 4600 ml.
Inspiratory capacity is the maximum amount of air a person can inhale after a normal exhalation. It is usually less than the vital capacity and should not be 5800 ml.

questions.explanation: Inspiratory capacity is the maximum amount of air a person can inhale after a normal exhalation. It is usually less than the vital capacity and should not be 5800 ml.

questions.text: What percentage of CO2 is carried in blood in combination with protien?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 75% → ❌ Incorrect. About 75% of CO₂ is carried as bicarbonate ions (HCO₃⁻), not with proteins.
50% → ❌ Incorrect. Half of CO₂ is not carried bound to proteins.

Carbon dioxide (CO2) is carried in the blood in three forms: dissolved CO2, bicarbonate ions (HCO3-), and carbamino compounds formed by combining with proteins (haemoglobin) in the blood. Of these, around 25% of CO2 is carried in combination with proteins, particularly haemoglobin. This helps in transporting CO2 from the tissues to the lungs for eventual exhalation.
0% → ❌ Incorrect. Some CO₂ does bind with proteins, so it is not zero.

questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is carried in the blood in three forms: dissolved CO2, bicarbonate ions (HCO3-), and carbamino compounds formed by combining with proteins (haemoglobin) in the blood. Of these, around 25% of CO2 is carried in combination with proteins, particularly haemoglobin. This helps in transporting CO2 from the tissues to the lungs for eventual exhalation.

questions.text: Which is not about the human respiratory surface?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The human respiratory surface needs to be moist to allow gases to dissolve before diffusing across.
A rich blood supply is crucial for the exchange of gases between the respiratory surface and blood vessels.
The presence of a concentration gradient is essential for gases to diffuse across the respiratory surface.

options.text: Surfactant on inner and outer side
options.explanation: This is incorrect because surfactant is typically present on the inner side of the respiratory surface (alveoli) to reduce surface tension and prevent alveolar collapse. It is not found on the outer side of the respiratory surface.

questions.explanation: This is incorrect because surfactant is typically present on the inner side of the respiratory surface (alveoli) to reduce surface tension and prevent alveolar collapse. It is not found on the outer side of the respiratory surface.

questions.text: It enters into lungs
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The trachea, or windpipe, is the tube that carries air from the larynx down to the primary bronchi, but it is not the direct entry into the lungs.
The oesophagus is part of the digestive system, transporting food from the mouth to the stomach, and does not play a role in air entering the lungs.
The primary bronchus is the first major airway that branches from the trachea and enters each lung, carrying air into the lungs.
The secondary bronchus further divides the primary bronchus once inside the lungs, distributing air to specific lung regions, but is not the initial entry point for air.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the primary bronchus, which is the first large branch that carries air directly into each lung. The trachea serves as a conduit for air to reach the primary bronchi but does not enter the lungs itself. The oesophagus is unrelated to the respiratory system as it is part of the digestive system. The secondary bronchus branches from the primary bronchus inside the lungs and distributes air further but is not the initial entry point for air into the lungs.

questions.text: All of the following will prevent the collapse of respiratory system except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cartilage provides structural support to the respiratory system, helping to maintain the airways' shape and preventing collapse.
Residual volume refers to the air that remains in the lungs after exhaling. While it doesn't prevent collapse directly, it contributes to maintaining lung function and preventing complete deflation.
Surfactant is a substance that reduces surface tension in the alveoli (air sacs), preventing them from collapsing during exhalation and ensuring efficient gas exchange.
Smooth muscles in the airways can contract and relax, regulating airflow and preventing collapse by adjusting the diameter of the air passages.

questions.explanation: Residual volume refers to the air that remains in the lungs after exhaling. While it doesn't prevent collapse directly, it contributes to maintaining lung function and preventing complete deflation.

questions.text: Voice box is another name used for
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The buccal cavity is the mouth where food is ingested and does not play a role in voice production.
The larynx, commonly known as the voice box, is crucial for sound production as it houses the vocal cords.
The trachea is the windpipe that connects the larynx to the lungs and is not involved in producing sound directly.
The pharynx is the throat region that facilitates the passage of air and food but does not produce sound.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the larynx. The larynx, located in the neck, is responsible for sound production because it contains the vocal cords, which vibrate to produce sound as air passes through. The buccal cavity (mouth) is involved in speech articulation but not in sound production. The trachea serves as a passage for air to reach the lungs, and the pharynx is involved in the passage of food and air but not in voice production.

questions.text: A disease of lungs in which inside of the lungs is damaged resulting in cough and fever
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lung cancer is characterized by the uncontrolled growth of abnormal cells in the lung tissues. While it can cause cough and fever in some cases, these symptoms are not specific to lung cancer.

Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition where the airways become inflamed and narrowed, leading to symptoms like wheezing and shortness of breath. Cough and fever are not primary symptoms of asthma.
Emphysema is a disease where the air sacs in the lungs are damaged, leading to breathing difficulties, coughing, and in severe cases, fever.

Tuberculosis (TB) is an infectious disease caused by bacteria that primarily affects the lungs. It can cause persistent cough, fever, and other symptoms, but it's not typically associated with damage to the inside of the lungs as described.

questions.explanation: Emphysema is a disease where the air sacs in the lungs are damaged, leading to breathing difficulties, coughing, and in severe cases, fever.

questions.text: Important factors which affect the capacity of haemoglobin to combine with oxygen is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: pH levels influence the binding of oxygen to hemoglobin. Specifically, lower pH (acidic conditions) facilitates the release of oxygen from hemoglobin, known as the Bohr effect. This is important in active tissues where acidity increases due to metabolic activity.
An increase in temperature can weaken the bond between oxygen and hemoglobin, making it easier for oxygen to be released in tissues where heat is generated, such as during intense physical activity.

Elevated levels of carbon dioxide can lead to a decrease in blood pH and promote the release of oxygen from hemoglobin. This ensures that oxygen is efficiently delivered to tissues with high metabolic rates.
All the mentioned factors—pH, temperature, and CO2 levels—affect the capacity of hemoglobin to combine with oxygen. Each plays a crucial role in ensuring oxygen is delivered where it's most needed.

questions.explanation: The capacity of hemoglobin to combine with oxygen is influenced by several key factors including pH, temperature, and CO2 levels. A lower pH (higher acidity) and higher CO2 levels both facilitate the release of oxygen from hemoglobin, adapting to the metabolic needs of tissues. Increased temperature also aids in releasing oxygen, allowing tissues with higher temperatures, such as muscles during exercise, to receive more oxygen. Therefore, all these factors collectively influence hemoglobin's oxygen-binding capacity. While each factor individually contributes to hemoglobin's behavior, they all work together to optimize oxygen delivery in different physiological conditions.

questions.text: Vocal cords are stretched across:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The vocal cords are housed within the larynx, also known as the voice box. This organ is crucial for phonation, as it allows for the vibration of the vocal cords, producing sound when air passes through them.

The glottis is the part of the larynx that consists of the vocal cords and the space between them. It is involved in producing sound, but it is not the structure across which the vocal cords are stretched; that structure is the larynx itself.

The pharynx is a muscular tube that serves as a pathway for air and food, connecting the nasal and oral cavities to the esophagus and larynx. It does not contain the vocal cords and is not directly involved in sound production.
The bronchi are extensions of the trachea that direct air into the lungs. They are part of the lower respiratory tract and do not play a role in sound production or contain the vocal cords.

questions.explanation: In the glottis (upper end of larynx), the mucous membrane is stretched across into two thin, edged fibrous bands called vocal cords, which help in sound production.

questions.text: About 70-85% of CO2 is carried as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A is correct because the majority of CO2 is carried in the blood as bicarbonate ions, a form of dissolved carbon dioxide.
While some CO2 dissolves directly in the blood, it's not the major way it's carried.
Carboxyhemoglobin refers to the binding of CO2 to hemoglobin, but it's not the primary way CO2 is carried in the blood.
While proteins like hemoglobin can help transport gases, CO2 is primarily carried as bicarbonate in the blood.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the majority of CO2 is carried in the blood as bicarbonate ions, a form of dissolved carbon dioxide.

questions.text: The mode of Respiration in mammals is :-
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mucosal refers to the lining of the respiratory tract and does not describe the mode of respiration.
Tracheal respiration is the mode of respiration used by insects, not mammals.
Cutaneous respiration involves breathing through the skin, but this is more common in certain amphibians, not mammals.
This option is correct because mammals primarily respire through their lungs, exchanging gases (oxygen and carbon dioxide) in a process called pulmonary respiration.

questions.explanation: This option is correct because mammals primarily respire through their lungs, exchanging gases (oxygen and carbon dioxide) in a process called pulmonary respiration.

questions.text: Site of gaseous exchange in humans is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Trachea is a long, U-shaped tube that connects your larynx (voice box) to your lungs. The trachea is often called the windpipe. It's a key part of your respiratory system. When you breathe in, air travels from your nose or mouth through your larynx.

The bronchi are the passageways that connect your windpipe to your lungs. You have two main bronchi in your right and left lungs that divide and branch off into smaller segments, like tree branches. At the end of your bronchi, the alveoli exchange oxygen and carbon dioxide.

Alveoli are tiny, balloon-like air sacs found at the end of the respiratory bronchioles and alveolar ducts in the lungs. They are the primary sites of gas exchange in the respiratory system. The alveoli are surrounded by an extensive network of pulmonary capillaries. Oxygen from the inhaled air diffuses across the thin walls of the alveoli into the bloodstream, and carbon dioxide, a waste product, diffuses from the bloodstream into the alveoli to be exhaled from the body.

Your nose is part of your respiratory system and performs several important jobs. It provides a sense of smell and filters dirt and allergens out of air as you breathe. Nasal symptoms such as a stuffy nose or nosebleed are common and usually not a reason for concern.

questions.explanation: Air sac is the functional unit of the lungs. Air sac consists of several microscopic single layered structures called alveoli. Gaseous exchange occurs at alveoli

questions.text: Gaseous exchange in animal take place with the help of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gaseous exchange in animals primarily takes place through the process of diffusion, where gases move from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration across a membrane.
Cyclosis refers to the movement of cytoplasm within a cell, not gaseous exchange.
Active transport involves the movement of molecules against a concentration gradient and requires energy. It is not the primary mechanism for gaseous exchange.
Phagocytosis is a process by which cells engulf solid particles, not gases.

questions.explanation: Gaseous exchange in animals primarily takes place through the process of diffusion, where gases move from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration across a membrane.

questions.text: Which of the following contain cartilaginous plates?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The primary bronchi are the two large air passages that branch off from the trachea, leading into each lung (the right and left primary bronchi). Like the trachea, they have C-shaped cartilage rings that provide rigidity and prevent collapse, but as they branch further into the lungs, the cartilage becomes more irregular and starts to form plates rather than rings. The primary bronchi also contain ciliated epithelial cells and mucus-secreting cells to help trap and expel foreign particles.

Secondary bronchi are branches of the primary bronchi and do contain cartilaginous plates. The secondary bronchi, also known as lobar bronchi, branch from the primary bronchi and enter the lobes of the lungs (three in the right lung and two in the left lung). At this level, the cartilage starts to change from complete rings to irregular cartilage plates. These plates provide structural support while allowing more flexibility for the bronchi to expand and contract during breathing. The epithelium continues to change as it branches further, transitioning from pseudostratified ciliated columnar to simpler epithelial types.
Bronchioles are smaller airways that do not have cartilaginous plates; their walls primarily consist of smooth muscle.

The trachea contains cartilaginous rings, not plates. The trachea, or windpipe, is a tube that connects the larynx (voice box) to the bronchi. It is composed of C-shaped rings of hyaline cartilage that provide structural support and keep the airway open during breathing. The open part of the "C" faces the esophagus, allowing it to expand during swallowing. The trachea is lined with a mucous membrane and cilia, which help trap and expel particles, keeping the airway clear.

questions.explanation: Secondary bronchi are branches of the primary bronchi and do contain cartilaginous plates. The secondary bronchi, also known as lobar bronchi, branch from the primary bronchi and enter the lobes of the lungs (three in the right lung and two in the left lung). At this level, the cartilage starts to change from complete rings to irregular cartilage plates. These plates provide structural support while allowing more flexibility for the bronchi to expand and contract during breathing. The epithelium continues to change as it branches further, transitioning from pseudostratified ciliated columnar to simpler epithelial types.
